tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2023 not reported and Tata FY2022-23 is consolidated

sections.about: This metric measures water consumption per tonne of crude steel or comparable physical output and is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water intensity in terms of physical output. It is the most operationally meaningful water metric for steel benchmarking because it normalises for production scale. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: JSW leads in FY2024 and FY2025, improving from 2.39 to 2.369 kL/tcs. SAIL worsens from 3.02 to 4.64 kL/tcs, narrowing the gap with Tata. Tata worsens from 4.40 to 4.76 kL/tcs, while Jindal remains highest at 7.36 kL/tcs in FY2025. Jindal’s FY2025 BRSR table confirms 7.36 KL/TCS and water consumption of 14.41 million kL, implying low absolute scale but higher per-tonne water use.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 3rd. JSW is best at 2.369 kL/tcs, making Tata’s 4.76 kL/tcs approximately 101% higher. Tata’s rank holds at 3rd from FY2024 to FY2025, but its own intensity worsens, indicating that production growth and water-efficiency measures did not offset consumption growth.
sections.targets: Tata’s water strategy identifies minimising water usage and maximising recycling. SAIL’s ZLD initiatives are relevant but not expressed as a kL/tcs target.
sections.comparabilityNotes: FY2022-23 physical-output intensity was not available for any company. This is the preferred metric for peer benchmarking versus absolute withdrawal/consumption.
